text: The surgeon performs a left lower lobe resection of the lung, using a bipolar fenestrated instrument in the left arm, a hook in the right arm, and a cat ear retractor in the posterior arm. The fissure is dissected to expose the pulmonary artery by opening the adventitia, revealing the high takeoff of the apical segment posteriorly and the basilar segment anteriorly.

The surgeon navigates around the apical segment, carefully sloping it while ensuring the descending thoracic aorta remains posterior. The basilar segmental arteries are sloped inferiorly to avoid the lingular artery, successfully managing both the apical segment and basilar segmental arteries together.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to staple the tissue while preserving the lingular artery. The lung is lifted to expose the vein, and dissection is performed in the inferior pulmonary ligament to harvest station line lymph nodes. The descending thoracic aorta is visible posteriorly, with the esophagus medial to it at the base of the pulmonary ligament. The pericardium is visible, with the underlying left ventricle beating, and the left atrium is seen proximal to the pulmonary vein.

The robotic system facilitates precise blunt and diathermal dissection of the posterior hiloprura and the adventitia of the vein. The surgeon uses a loop to carefully tease off the tissue, with magnification providing a detailed view.

The surgeon articulates the wristed instruments smoothly, introducing the stapler posteriorly through two twelve millimeter ports for flexible access. The lung is flipped back down, and the lower lobe bronchus is isolated. Lymph nodes are dissected, and the bronchus is stapled.
An endocatch bag is introduced through the anterior port to remove the left lower lobe along with the specimen.
